title: What Did I Learn About Money Before Age 10?
subtitle: A journal prompt set for mapping childhood money beliefs before deciding which ones to pass on
excerpt: A journal set for parents exploring what money beliefs they absorbed before they had words for them — and what they want their children to absorb instead. Five prompts moving from what was taken in to what is being chosen going forward.
sourceNotes: Original journal prompt set written for homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: Use when working on generational money patterns, particularly when parenting decisions about money trigger anxiety or guilt.

content.text: A journal set for mapping the money education you received before you knew you were receiving one. The prompts move from what was taken in early to what is being chosen now.
What is your earliest money memory?
Write it as a scene: where you were, who was there, what happened. Don't analyse it yet. Just put it on the page.
What feeling did that memory carry? Does it still?
Notice the gap, if any, between what you intellectually know and what you still carry in the gut.
What was money talk like in the house you grew up in?
Was it spoken or silent? Anxious or matter-of-fact? Which of those patterns are you still running?
What money messages are you passing on to your children — knowingly or not?
Name one thing you say or don't say about money that you heard from your own parents.
What do you want your children to know about money before they turn ten?
Write it as three sentences. Simple, specific, sayable. These are the sentences to start practising now.
